rules.rule: Use a native `Button` for built-in VoiceOver semantics, the `.isButton` trait, and Switch Control / external-keyboard activation.
rules.static_hint: The interactive element is implemented as a `Button` (or a control vending button semantics), not `Text`/`Image` with `.onTapGesture` or a bare `.gesture`.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the element is queryable via `.buttons[...]`, exposes the button trait in the accessibility tree, and `.tap()` fires the action.

rules.rule: The button has an accessible name that describes its purpose or action.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does the button's accessible name (visible title or `.accessibilityLabel`) describe what the button does, rather than what it looks like or nothing at all?
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the button element's `label` is non-empty in the accessibility tree.

rules.rule: When the button has visible text, the visible text serves as the accessible name.
rules.static_hint: A `Button` initialized with a text title carries no `.accessibilityLabel` override that replaces the visible text.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the button's `label` equals its visible title text.

rules.rule: When additional context is needed beyond the visible text, add it via `.accessibilityLabel`.
rules.static_hint: An `.accessibilityLabel` modifier is present on the `Button` where a contextual label is required.
rules.llm_eval_question: Is the visible text alone ambiguous in this screen's context, and if so, does an `.accessibilityLabel` supply the missing context?

rules.rule: The visible text appears at the start of the `.accessibilityLabel`.
rules.static_hint: When both the button title and `.accessibilityLabel` are string literals, the label string has the title string as its prefix.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does the `.accessibilityLabel` begin with the button's visible text before adding context?

rules.rule: For icon-only buttons, provide an accessible name via `.accessibilityLabel`.
rules.static_hint: Any `Button` whose only content is an `Image` (e.g., `Image(systemName:)`) carries an `.accessibilityLabel` or an accessible `Label` initializer.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the icon-only button's `label` is non-empty and is not the raw SF Symbol name.

rules.rule: When multiple buttons on the same screen share visible text (e.g., a repeated "Edit" button per row), give each one a unique, specific `.accessibilityLabel` (e.g., "Edit Username") so VoiceOver users can distinguish them.
rules.llm_eval_question: Where buttons repeat visible text, does each `.accessibilityLabel` add context that genuinely distinguishes the targets (e.g., "Edit Username" vs. "Edit Password")?
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: query all buttons on the rendered screen and assert no two repeated-action buttons expose identical `label` values.

rules.rule: Keep icons inside a button decorative to VoiceOver.
rules.static_hint: No `.accessibilityLabel`, `.accessibilityAddTraits`, or un-hiding modifiers are applied to the `Image` inside the `Button`.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the button is a single accessibility element; the icon does not appear as a separately focusable image element.

rules.rule: `Image(systemName:)` inside a `Button` is decorative by default; label the button as a whole rather than adding a separate `.accessibilityLabel` to the icon.
rules.static_hint: `.accessibilityLabel` is attached to the `Button` (or its `Label`), not to the inner `Image`.

rules.rule: If the action is unavailable, disable the button with `.disabled(true)` so it becomes non-interactive and is announced as dimmed.
rules.llm_eval_question: When the code models the action as unavailable, is that state wired to `.disabled(...)` rather than to an opacity change or a no-op handler?
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: with the action unavailable, the button's `isEnabled` is false and tapping does not fire the action.
rules.device_human_check: VoiceOver on a physical device announces the button with "dimmed" appended while it is disabled.

rules.rule: Meets the touch target size baseline in `global_rules.md` (`global.touch-target-size`).
rules.static_hint: No explicit `.frame`/padding math yields an interactive area smaller than 44×44 pt (e.g., small fixed frames on icon buttons without compensating padding or `.contentShape`).
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the button's `frame` is at least 44×44 pt, and `performAccessibilityAudit(for: .hitRegion)` reports no hit-region issues.

rules.rule: Meets the system focus indicator baseline in `global_rules.md` (`global.focus-visible`).
rules.static_hint: No custom `ButtonStyle` or modifier suppresses or clips the system focus indicator (e.g., focus effects disabled or the focus ring clipped by a container).
rules.device_human_check: With Full Keyboard Access (and VoiceOver) on a physical device, moving focus to the button shows the system focus indicator clearly in both light and dark mode.

rules.rule: Do not include the word "Button" in `.accessibilityLabel`. VoiceOver already appends the "Button" trait, so this makes it announce "Button, Button".
rules.static_hint: `.accessibilityLabel` string literals on buttons do not contain the word "button" (case-insensitive).
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: no button element's `label` in the accessibility tree contains the word "Button".

rules.rule: Do not create icon-only buttons without an `.accessibilityLabel` (no unlabeled icons).
rules.static_hint: Flag any `Button` whose content is only an `Image` and that lacks an `.accessibilityLabel` (or accessible `Label` initializer).
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: no button in the tree has an empty `label` or a raw SF Symbol identifier as its `label`.

rules.rule: Do not use an `.accessibilityLabel` that conflicts with (or is wildly different from) the visible label text.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does the `.accessibilityLabel` say essentially the same thing as the visible text (plus context), rather than something conflicting or unrelated?

rules.rule: Accessible names should at least begin with the visible label.
rules.static_hint: When both the visible title and `.accessibilityLabel` are string literals, the label has the visible title as its prefix.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does the accessible name begin with the visible label text?

rules.rule: Use a native `Button` for built-in VoiceOver semantics and Switch Control / external-keyboard activation.
rules.static_hint: The toggle control is implemented as a `Button`, not `Text`/`Image` with `.onTapGesture` or a bare `.gesture`.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the element is queryable via `.buttons[...]`, exposes the button trait, and `.tap()` fires the action.

rules.rule: The button has an accessible name that describes its purpose or action.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does the toggle button's accessible name describe what activating it does, rather than what it looks like?
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the button element's `label` is non-empty in the accessibility tree.

rules.rule: Default strategy: represent state by changing the accessible name to the next action (e.g., "Mute" to "Unmute", "Pin" to "Remove Pin") via `.accessibilityLabel`.
rules.static_hint: The `.accessibilityLabel` (or button title) is a state-conditional expression, not a constant string.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does each state's accessible name describe the action a tap will perform next (e.g., "Mute" while unmuted), not the current state?
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: read the button's `label`, tap it, and assert the `label` changed to the opposite action.

rules.rule: When the button has visible text and no override is needed, the visible text serves as the accessible name.
rules.static_hint: A `Button` with a state-driven visible text title carries no redundant `.accessibilityLabel` override diverging from it.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the button's `label` equals its visible title text in each state.

rules.rule: For icon-only toggle buttons, provide an accessible name via `.accessibilityLabel` that reflects the current state's next action.
rules.static_hint: An icon-only toggle `Button` has a state-conditional `.accessibilityLabel`.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does the label name the next action for the current state, rather than the icon glyph or the current state?
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the button's `label` is non-empty and flips to the opposite action after tapping.

rules.rule: Keep icons inside a toggle button decorative to VoiceOver.
rules.static_hint: No `.accessibilityLabel`, `.accessibilityAddTraits`, or un-hiding modifiers are applied to the `Image` inside the toggle `Button`.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the toggle button is a single accessibility element; the icon does not appear as a separately focusable image element.

rules.rule: Label the button as a whole, not the icon.
rules.static_hint: `.accessibilityLabel` is attached to the `Button` (or its `Label`), not to the inner `Image`.

rules.rule: If the action is unavailable, disable the button with `.disabled(true)`.
rules.llm_eval_question: When the code models the action as unavailable, is that state wired to `.disabled(...)` rather than to an opacity change or a no-op handler?
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: with the action unavailable, the button's `isEnabled` is false and tapping does not fire the action.

rules.rule: Meets the touch target size baseline in `global_rules.md` (`global.touch-target-size`).
rules.static_hint: No explicit `.frame`/padding math yields an interactive area smaller than 44×44 pt, particularly on icon-only toolbar toggles.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the button's `frame` is at least 44×44 pt, and `performAccessibilityAudit(for: .hitRegion)` reports no hit-region issues.

rules.rule: Meets the system focus indicator baseline in `global_rules.md` (`global.focus-visible`).
rules.static_hint: No custom style or modifier suppresses or clips the system focus indicator.
rules.device_human_check: With Full Keyboard Access (and VoiceOver) on a physical device, moving focus to the toggle shows the system focus indicator clearly in both light and dark mode.

rules.rule: (Formatting toolbar exception) If the control is a formatting toggle in a toolbar (e.g., Bold/Italic/Underline), keep the accessible name stable (e.g., "Bold").
rules.static_hint: The button's title/`.accessibilityLabel` is a constant, not conditional on the toggle state.
rules.llm_eval_question: Is this control a formatting toggle in a toolbar, and is its accessible name a stable, state-independent name (e.g., "Bold")?
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: toggle the control and assert its `label` is identical before and after.

rules.rule: (Formatting toolbar exception) Convey the pressed state with `.accessibilityAddTraits(isActive ? .isSelected : [])` instead of renaming it. VoiceOver appends "Selected" after the accessible name while the trait is present.
rules.static_hint: `.accessibilityAddTraits` applies `.isSelected` conditionally on the live toggle state, and the label expression is not state-conditional.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: `isSelected` is true on the element when active and absent when inactive, verified across a tap.
rules.device_human_check: VoiceOver on device announces "Selected" after the stable name when active, and omits it when inactive.

rules.rule: (Formatting toolbar exception) SwiftUI has no "not selected" trait. When the toggle is inactive, omit `.isSelected` rather than adding an antonym trait.
rules.static_hint: The inactive branch of the trait conditional resolves to an empty trait set; no antonym/"not selected" trait workaround is applied.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: when inactive, the element exposes no selected state and no substitute trait.

rules.rule: (Formatting toolbar exception) Add `.accessibilityValue("Not Selected")` for the inactive state only when the off state would otherwise be undiscoverable (the name is stable across states, the control is rendered in isolation rather than in a visually grouped set of peer toggles, and no other accessible signal exposes the state).
rules.static_hint: `.accessibilityValue("Not Selected")` appears only in the inactive branch, never unconditionally.
rules.llm_eval_question: If `.accessibilityValue("Not Selected")` is present, do all three undiscoverability conditions hold? If it is absent, is the off state still discoverable through another accessible signal?
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: when the exception applies, the inactive element's `value` reads "Not Selected" and the active element carries no such value.

rules.rule: Do not add the `.isSelected` trait to a toggle button that is also changing its accessible name to the next action. Pick one state model, not both; combining them produces conflicting, confusing announcements like "Unmute, Selected".
rules.static_hint: Flag any `Button` that has both a state-conditional label/title and a conditional `.isSelected` trait.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: assert it is not the case that the element's `label` flips after tapping and the element also toggles `isSelected`.

rules.rule: Do not leave `.isSelected` stale, always applied, or never applied when using the toolbar trait strategy; it must track the live state.
rules.static_hint: `.accessibilityAddTraits(.isSelected)` is conditioned on the state variable, never applied as a constant.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: toggle repeatedly; `isSelected` in the accessibility tree matches the actual control state after every activation.

rules.rule: Do not ship icon-only toggle buttons without an accessible name.
rules.static_hint: Flag any icon-only toggle `Button` lacking an `.accessibilityLabel` (or accessible `Label` initializer).
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: no toggle button in the tree has an empty `label` or a raw SF Symbol identifier as its `label`.

rules.rule: Do not put state only in the icon's visual appearance. Screen reader users must get the state via the accessible-name change or the `.isSelected` trait, depending on which strategy is in use.
rules.static_hint: The state variable feeds an `.accessibilityLabel` change or a conditional `.isSelected` trait, not only the `Image` name, color, or fill variant.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: after tapping, either the element's `label` changes or `isSelected` toggles — some accessible state signal changes, not just the rendered icon.

rules.rule: Use the native `Toggle` control rather than a custom composition.
rules.static_hint: The control is a `Toggle`, not a hand-rolled `HStack`/shape/gesture composition.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the element appears as a single switch (`.switches`) exposing an on/off `value` (1/0).

rules.rule: Pass the label directly into the initializer (e.g., `Toggle("Enable notifications", isOn: $binding)`) so the label, switch role, and on/off state merge into a single accessible element.
rules.static_hint: The label is supplied via the `Toggle` initializer (title string or label view), not as a sibling `Text` next to a label-less `Toggle`.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: one accessibility element exposes both the name and the on/off value; the label is not a separate static-text element.

rules.rule: The switch has an accessible name that describes the setting. Often this is worded to be true when the switch is on (e.g., "Enable notifications").
rules.llm_eval_question: Does the accessible name describe the setting being controlled, ideally phrased so it is true when the switch is on?
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the switch element's `label` is non-empty in the accessibility tree.

rules.rule: When there is no unique visible label (e.g., repeated switches in a list sharing generic visual text), give each `Toggle` a unique, specific `.accessibilityLabel`.
rules.llm_eval_question: Where switches repeat generic visible text, does each `.accessibilityLabel` name the specific setting that switch controls?
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: query all switches on the rendered screen; no two expose identical `label` values.

rules.rule: When the on/off state is represented by visible value text other than "On"/"Off" (e.g., "Allowed"/"Blocked", "Dark"/"Light"), set `.accessibilityValue` to match so VoiceOver announces the correct state instead of defaulting to generic On/Off wording.
rules.static_hint: A state-conditional `.accessibilityValue` modifier is present whenever custom value wording is rendered.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does the visible state wording differ from On/Off, and if so, does `.accessibilityValue` mirror the visible wording in both states?
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: in each state, the switch's `value` matches the custom visible wording (e.g., "Allowed"/"Blocked"), not 1/0.

rules.rule: The off-state fill maintains at least 3:1 contrast against the adjacent background, so the off state is distinguishable by more than color (a plain light gray is a common offender).
rules.static_hint: When the off-state tint and adjacent background resolve to concrete color values (hex literals or asset-catalog tokens), compute the contrast ratio and require ≥3:1.
rules.device_human_check: On a physical device in both light and dark mode, verify the off-state fill measures ≥3:1 against the adjacent background and the off state reads without relying on hue.

rules.rule: Meets the touch target size baseline in `global_rules.md` (`global.touch-target-size`).
rules.static_hint: No `.scaleEffect`/`.frame` shrinking reduces the switch's interactive area below 44×44 pt.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the switch's `frame` is at least 44×44 pt, and `performAccessibilityAudit(for: .hitRegion)` reports no hit-region issues.

rules.rule: Meets the system focus indicator baseline in `global_rules.md` (`global.focus-visible`).
rules.static_hint: No custom toggle style or modifier suppresses or clips the system focus indicator.
rules.device_human_check: With Full Keyboard Access (and VoiceOver) on a physical device, moving focus to the switch shows the system focus indicator clearly in both light and dark mode.

rules.rule: Do not build a switch out of a custom `HStack` of `Text` plus a shape-based knob without grouping it. Without an explicit `Toggle` (or `.accessibilityElement(children: .combine)`), the label and the control become two disconnected accessibility elements instead of one combined name-and-state announcement.
rules.static_hint: Flag an `HStack` of `Text` plus a shape/knob with a tap gesture that has neither a `Toggle` nor `.accessibilityElement(children: .combine)`.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: the switch UI is one accessibility element carrying both name and state, not a text element plus a separate unnamed control.

rules.rule: Do not leave `.accessibilityValue` mismatched with the visible value text when the switch shows custom state wording instead of On/Off.
rules.static_hint: When the visible value strings and `.accessibilityValue` strings are literals, they match per state.
rules.llm_eval_question: In each state, does `.accessibilityValue` say the same thing as the visible value text rendered for that state?
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest: toggle through both states; the element's `value` equals the visible value text shown for each state.

rules.rule: Do not rely on color alone (e.g., green vs. gray) to convey on/off state; the off state must remain distinguishable through sufficient contrast.
rules.static_hint: Where colors are literal or token-resolvable, flag off-state fills that fall below 3:1 against the adjacent background (gray-on-light-gray is the classic offender).
rules.device_human_check: On a physical device in light and dark mode, confirm the on/off states are distinguishable without hue — the off-state fill meets ≥3:1 against its background.

rules.rule: Any control drawn from primitives (shapes, `Canvas`, `Path`, raw gestures) rather than a native control exposes a hidden native equivalent through `.accessibilityRepresentation { }`
rules.static_hint: Interactive views built from Shape/Canvas/Path/gesture primitives carry .accessibilityRepresentation { } in their modifier chain.
rules.llm_eval_question: Is this primitive-drawn view actually a control (something the user operates), so that it requires a native representation?
rules.runtime_check: The element appears in the XCUITest accessibility tree with the represented native control's role and traits.

rules.rule: supplying an accessible name
rules.static_hint: The representation's native control is constructed with a non-empty label.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does the supplied name describe the control's purpose?
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest reads a non-empty label on the element.

rules.rule: the current value (for a slider, stepper, or toggle)
rules.static_hint: The representation binds the control's live value (e.g., Slider(value: $x)) rather than a constant.
rules.runtime_check: The element's accessibility value matches the visible state and updates after adjustment.

rules.rule: the correct role and trait
rules.llm_eval_question: Does the chosen native representation's role match the interaction the custom control actually performs (slider for drag-adjust, toggle for binary state, button for tap)?
rules.runtime_check: The element exposes the expected traits in the accessibility tree (e.g., .adjustable for a slider representation); the .trait audit reports no failures.

rules.rule: Expose the control's actions through the `.accessibilityRepresentation` (WCAG 2.2 A 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value).
rules.static_hint: The representation wires real handlers and bindings (button action, toggle binding), not empty closures.
rules.runtime_check: Activating or adjusting the element via XCUITest fires the same state change as direct touch.
rules.device_human_check: VoiceOver double-tap and swipe-adjust gestures operate the control.

rules.rule: Bind the representation and the visible control to the same state so the announced value stays in sync as the user interacts.
rules.static_hint: The representation and the drawn control reference the same @State/@Binding source; flag duplicated state or constant bindings.
rules.runtime_check: After interacting, the element's accessibility value matches the rendered state.
rules.device_human_check: VoiceOver announces the updated value immediately after each adjustment.

rules.rule: For a control adjusted by dragging, add `.accessibilityAddTraits(.allowsDirectInteraction)` so a VoiceOver user can move it directly.
rules.static_hint: Drag-gesture-driven controls carry .accessibilityAddTraits(.allowsDirectInteraction).
rules.llm_eval_question: Is this control genuinely adjusted by continuous dragging (needing direct interaction), rather than by discrete taps?
rules.device_human_check: With VoiceOver running on a device, the user can drag the control directly and it responds.

rules.rule: Do not ship a custom-drawn control with only a tap gesture and a visual label; it is unreachable and inoperable for VoiceOver, Switch Control, and Full Keyboard Access users.
rules.static_hint: Flag Shape/Canvas/Path views with .onTapGesture and no accessibility modifiers (no .accessibilityRepresentation, no label + traits + actions).
rules.runtime_check: The .sufficientElementDescription and .trait audits fail, or the element is missing/unlabeled in the XCUITest accessibility tree.
rules.device_human_check: VoiceOver cannot reach or activate the control; Switch Control and Full Keyboard Access skip it.

rules.rule: Do not hand-rebuild the contract with loose `.accessibilityLabel`, `.accessibilityValue`, and traits when a native representation supplies the role behaviors (adjustable actions, toggling) that loose traits alone do not.
rules.static_hint: Flag custom controls carrying .accessibilityLabel/.accessibilityValue/.accessibilityAddTraits stacks with no .accessibilityRepresentation or .accessibilityAdjustableAction.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does this hand-rebuilt stack actually deliver the role behaviors (adjustability, toggle semantics) a native representation would supply, or does it only announce them without implementing them?
rules.device_human_check: With VoiceOver, swipe-up/down adjust and double-tap toggle behave exactly as they would on the native control.

rules.rule: Use a built-in text style (`.body`, `.headline`, `.caption`) or a `Text` with no fixed point size, so text scales with the user's setting (WCAG 2.1 AA 1.4.4 Resize Text).
rules.static_hint: Text uses .font(.body/.headline/...) or no font modifier; flag .font(.system(size:)) and Font.custom without relativeTo:.
rules.runtime_check: The .dynamicType audit in performAccessibilityAudit() reports no failures across type sizes.

rules.rule: Size any glyph-tracking dimension (icon size, control height, glyph padding) with `@ScaledMetric(relativeTo:)` rather than a fixed point value.
rules.static_hint: Flag fixed point values in .frame/.padding on icon and control layouts adjacent to text; require @ScaledMetric(relativeTo:)-backed values.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does this fixed dimension track a glyph (an icon beside text, a control height wrapping a label, glyph padding), making it a @ScaledMetric candidate, or is it genuinely layout-independent?
rules.runtime_check: In XCUITest at an accessibility text size, confirm the icon/control dimensions grew with the text rather than staying fixed.

rules.rule: Let text wrap and containers grow
rules.static_hint: Flag truncating .lineLimit and fixed-height frames on content text; containers should size intrinsically.
rules.runtime_check: The .textClipped audit reports no failures at enlarged type sizes.
rules.device_human_check: At the largest accessibility text size, text wraps and containers expand with no clipping or overlap on any screen.

rules.rule: Place long content in a `ScrollView`.
rules.static_hint: Screens whose content can exceed the viewport wrap it in ScrollView/List; flag fixed VStacks holding unbounded text.
rules.llm_eval_question: Is this content long (can exceed the viewport at accessibility sizes) and therefore required to live in a ScrollView?
rules.device_human_check: At the largest accessibility size, all content remains reachable by scrolling.

rules.rule: For a `TextField` that can hold long input, use `axis: .vertical`.
rules.static_hint: TextField initializers for free-form fields include axis: .vertical.
rules.llm_eval_question: Can this field plausibly hold long input (notes, descriptions, addresses) such that it needs axis: .vertical, or is it inherently short (a code, a quantity)?

rules.rule: Do not set a fixed `.font(.system(size:))` on content text.
rules.static_hint: AST/grep for .font(.system(size:)) — and Font.custom(_, size:) without relativeTo: — applied to Text.
rules.llm_eval_question: Is this fixed-size text content the user must read (violation), rather than a rare decorative glyph where fixed sizing could be acceptable?

rules.rule: Do not apply a truncating `.lineLimit()` to meaningful text
rules.static_hint: Flag .lineLimit(n) applied to Text holding content.
rules.llm_eval_question: Is the truncated text meaningful content the user must read in full, or a deliberate preview/teaser pattern with a path to the full content?
rules.runtime_check: The .textClipped audit flags text clipped at enlarged sizes.

rules.rule: Do not wrap meaningful text in a fixed-height frame that clips it when enlarged.
rules.static_hint: Flag .frame(height:)/.frame(maxHeight:) with clipping on containers holding Text.
rules.runtime_check: The .textClipped audit reports no failures at accessibility text sizes.

rules.rule: Do not clamp the app's Dynamic Type range to protect a layout; fix the layout instead.
rules.static_hint: Grep/AST for .dynamicTypeSize(...) range clamps (e.g., ...DynamicTypeSize.large) and app-level maximumContentSizeCategory overrides.
rules.llm_eval_question: Is this Dynamic Type clamp protecting a layout (violation) rather than a documented, justified exception?

rules.rule: Every interactive control shows a visible system focus indicator for Full Keyboard Access, external-keyboard, and Switch Control users. The default `Button`, `Toggle`, and other native control styles preserve this automatically.
rules.static_hint: Verify each interactive element is a native control (Button, Toggle, Picker, etc.) rendered through a system control style with no focus-suppressing custom style — native usage is the pass condition, since no audit covers focus-indicator visibility.
rules.device_human_check: With Full Keyboard Access (and separately Switch Control) enabled on a device, tab/scan to every control and confirm a visible focus indicator appears.

rules.rule: Do not apply a custom style modifier (e.g., a `ButtonStyle` or `ToggleStyle` built with `PlainButtonStyle` or a bespoke shape) that suppresses the system focus indicator without restoring an equivalent visible focus treatment.
rules.static_hint: Flag .buttonStyle(.plain)/PlainButtonStyle and bespoke ButtonStyle/ToggleStyle conformances applied to interactive controls as review candidates.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does this custom style's source restore a visible focus treatment (e.g., drawing on focus state) equivalent to the system indicator it replaces?
rules.device_human_check: With Full Keyboard Access on a device, confirm the custom-styled control still shows a visible focus indicator when focused.

rules.rule: Set a `navigationTitle` on every pushed screen so its screen change is announced by name (WCAG 2.2 A 2.4.3 Focus Order).
rules.static_hint: Every navigationDestination/NavigationLink destination view sets .navigationTitle.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest asserts the navigation bar exists with a non-empty title after each push.
rules.device_human_check: VoiceOver announces the new screen by name on push.

rules.rule: Let the system manage VoiceOver focus on a `NavigationStack` push; do not force focus to the back button or an arbitrary element.
rules.static_hint: Flag @AccessibilityFocusState writes in the onAppear/task of pushed screens, and UIAccessibility.post(notification: .screenChanged, argument:) calls carrying a target element.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does any focus manipulation on push override the system's placement, rather than serving a documented exception?
rules.device_human_check: On device, pushing a screen moves VoiceOver focus per the system default — not yanked to the back button or an arbitrary element.

rules.rule: On pop, restore focus to the row that triggered the navigation by binding it with `@AccessibilityFocusState` and setting that focus when the pushed screen is dismissed, where the system does not restore it.
rules.static_hint: Rows that trigger navigation bind @AccessibilityFocusState, and the code sets that focus when the pushed screen is dismissed.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does the restore target the specific row that triggered navigation, and is it applied only where the system fails to restore focus on its own?
rules.device_human_check: On a physical device (the Simulator does not honor @AccessibilityFocusState), popping returns VoiceOver focus to the triggering row.

rules.rule: Do not push a screen with no title, which leaves the change without a clear spoken announcement.
rules.static_hint: Flag pushed destination views lacking .navigationTitle.
rules.runtime_check: XCUITest finds an empty or missing navigation-bar title after push.
rules.device_human_check: VoiceOver's push announcement names the screen rather than announcing an unnamed change.

rules.rule: Do not override the system's push-focus placement.
rules.static_hint: Flag focus-forcing on push: @AccessibilityFocusState set in a pushed screen's onAppear, or .screenChanged notifications posted with a target element.
rules.llm_eval_question: Does this code path change where VoiceOver focus lands on push compared to the system default?
rules.device_human_check: On device, VoiceOver focus lands where the system places it after each push.

rules.rule: Use semantic color for text and control surfaces: the design system's color tokens, or SwiftUI system colors (`.primary`, `.secondary`, `Color(.label)`, `Color(.systemBackground)`, `.tint`). These adapt to light and dark mode and are pre-vetted for contrast.
rules.static_hint: Allowlist check: colors applied to text and control chrome resolve to design-system tokens or SwiftUI semantic colors, not literals.
rules.llm_eval_question: Is every color applied to a text or control surface (as opposed to purely decorative art) drawn from the token set or system semantic colors?

rules.rule: Use native control styles (`.borderedProminent`, `.roundedBorder`, the system `Toggle`) so a control's border, track, and state fills inherit their contrast instead of being hand-drawn.
rules.static_hint: Controls use system styles (.borderedProminent, .roundedBorder, default Toggle); flag hand-drawn control chrome (shapes/strokes composing a control's border or track).
rules.llm_eval_question: Where control chrome is drawn by hand, is there a reason a native style could not serve, or is this a needless rebuild that forfeits inherited contrast?

rules.rule: Give any custom-drawn indicator (a selection outline, a custom toggle track) a semantic or design-token color rather than a fixed literal.
rules.static_hint: Flag fixed literals (Color.black/.white, Color(white:), hex/RGB initializers) on Shape fills and strokes; require token or semantic colors.
rules.llm_eval_question: Is this drawn element a meaningful indicator (selection outline, toggle track) — and therefore required to use a semantic/token color — rather than decoration?

rules.rule: Do not hardcode raw colors (`Color.black`, `Color.white`, or gray literals like `Color(white: 0.7)`) for text or control chrome; they do not adapt to the color scheme and often fail contrast.
rules.static_hint: Grep/AST for Color.black, Color.white, Color(white:), Color(red:green:blue:) applied via foregroundColor/foregroundStyle/tint/background to text or controls.
rules.llm_eval_question: Is the literal color applied to text or control chrome (violation) rather than to a decorative element where a fixed color may be acceptable?
rules.runtime_check: The .contrast audit in performAccessibilityAudit() catches downstream text-contrast failures, run in both light and dark mode.

rules.rule: Do not restyle a native control in a way that strips its default border, track, or state contrast.
rules.static_hint: Flag restyling modifiers on native controls (custom ToggleStyle/ButtonStyle, .tint with low-contrast values, .background/.border overrides) as review candidates.
rules.llm_eval_question: After this restyling, do the control's border, track, and state fills still carry their default contrast, or has the style stripped them?
rules.device_human_check: Measure the restyled control's border/track/state-fill contrast against adjacent colors in light and dark mode (3:1 minimum, WCAG 1.4.11) — non-text contrast has no automated audit.

rules.rule: Do not convey a control's state (on/off, selected) by color alone; keep a shape, fill, or label difference as well.
rules.static_hint: Heuristic: flag state-conditional expressions whose branches differ only in a color argument (e.g., foregroundColor(isOn ? a : b)) with no accompanying shape, fill, icon, or label change.
rules.llm_eval_question: When this control changes state, does anything other than color change — a shape, fill, icon, or label?
rules.device_human_check: With grayscale color filters enabled on the device, confirm the on/off or selected state is still distinguishable.

rules.rule: Every tappable control has a hit area of at least 24x24 points (WCAG 2.2 AA, 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum)).
rules.static_hint: Flag tappable elements (Button, .onTapGesture) carrying an explicit .frame smaller than 24x24; absence of small fixed frames is only a weak pass — the actual hit area needs runtime measurement.
rules.runtime_check: performAccessibilityAudit(for: .hitRegion) reports no failures; XCUITest asserts each tappable element's frame is at least 24x24 points.

rules.rule: Inline targets within a run of text are exempt from this minimum.
rules.static_hint: Detect inline-link constructs (Markdown links inside Text, AttributedString .link runs) to mark candidates for the exemption.
rules.llm_eval_question: Is this small target genuinely an inline link within a run of text (exempt), rather than a standalone control that merely renders as text?

rules.rule: For icon-only controls where the visible glyph is smaller than 24x24, use `.frame(minWidth: 24, minHeight: 24)` on the tappable element to extend the hit area without resizing the glyph.
rules.static_hint: For Buttons whose label is only an Image/systemImage, require .frame(minWidth: 24, minHeight: 24) (or larger) on the tappable element.
rules.runtime_check: Assert the icon-only control's element frame is at least 24x24 while the rendered glyph keeps its designed size; the .hitRegion audit passes.

rules.rule: Prefer a 44x44 point hit area where layout allows, matching Apple's Human Interface Guidelines. Treat 44x44 as the target and 24x24 as the non-negotiable floor.
rules.static_hint: Advisory lint only: flag tappable frames sized between 24 and 44 points for review — a linter cannot decide whether layout allows 44.
rules.llm_eval_question: Where the layout has room, does this control extend its hit area to 44x44 — and if it stops short, is there a real layout constraint justifying it?
rules.runtime_check: Measure element frames in XCUITest: report controls between 24x24 and 44x44 as advisory; fail anything under 24x24.

rules.rule: Do not rely on the visible glyph size alone to satisfy the minimum; extend the frame, not the icon.
rules.static_hint: Flag icon-only tappables whose only sizing is a font or resizable modifier on the glyph (e.g., .font(.system(size: 28))) with no .frame or .contentShape extending the hit area.
rules.runtime_check: Assert the tappable element's frame — not just the rendered image size — meets the floor via the .hitRegion audit and frame measurement.
